data.title: Nike Shares Edge Higher Tuesday: What's Behind The Strength?
data.description:
Nike Inc (NYSE:NKE) shares are trading marginally higher on Tuesday, gaining despite the company progressing more slowly than anticipated in its turnaround strategy. This movement occurs while major indices are experiencing declines, with the S&P 500 down 0.15% and the Dow Jones falling 0.60%. Here’s what investors need to know.

  • Nike shares are advancing steadily. Why is NKE stock advancing?

Is Nike's Aggressive Strategy Backfiring?

Needham recently downgraded Nike’s rating from Buy to Hold while keeping the price target unchanged at $68. The analyst highlighted that the company’s aggressive selling to the North American wholesale channel could be a “potential strategic misstep,” suggesting that the current brand heat may not support the level of sell-in.

Additionally, challenges in China and the Converse brand are intensifying, with the timeline for recovery appearing uncertain. The consensus estimates for Nike’s recovery seem overly optimistic, indicating potential downside to current projections.

The broader market is experiencing a mixed day, with major indexes falling Tuesday. In contrast, Nike’s stock is moving slightly higher despite the overall market’s downward trend, suggesting that company-specific factors are influencing its performance.

The Surprising Strength Behind Recent Stock Gains

Nike is currently trading 4.7% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 4.3% above its 50-day SMA, indicating short-term strength. However, it is trading 2% below its 100-day SMA and 0.3% below its 200-day SMA, suggesting some longer-term weakness. Shares have decreased by 8.20% over the past 12 months and are currently positioned closer to their 52-week highs than lows.

The RSI is at 55.36, which is considered neutral territory, indicating that the stock is neither overbought nor oversold. Meanwhile, MACD is above its signal line, suggesting bullish momentum.

The combination of neutral RSI and bullish MACD suggests mixed momentum.

  • Key Resistance: $66.50
  • Key Support: $63.00

The Valuation Puzzle: High Premium Or Justified?

Investors are looking ahead to the next earnings report on March 19.

  • EPS Estimate: 31 cents (Down from 54 cents YoY)
  • Revenue Estimate: $11.24 billion (Down from $11.27 billion YoY)
  • Valuation: P/E of 38.4x (Indicates premium valuation)

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $77.13. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Needham: Upgraded to Hold
  • RBC Capital: Outperform (Lowered Target to $78.00)
  • UBS: Neutral (Lowered Target to $62.00)

Valuation Insight: While the stock trades at a premium P/E multiple, the strong consensus and 43% expected earnings decline suggest analysts view this growth as justification for the 16% upside to analyst targets.

Benzinga Edge Rankings

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for Nike, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Value: Neutral (Score: 54.41/100) — The stock is trading at a premium relative to peers.
  • Quality: Weak (Score: 16.26/100) — Indicates potential concerns regarding financial health.
  • Momentum: Weak (Score: 15.13/100) — The stock is underperforming the broader market.

The Verdict: Nike’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a mixed outlook. While the Value score indicates some premium pricing, the weak Quality and Momentum scores suggest caution for investors.

Top ETF Exposure

  • The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSE:XLY): 2.21% Weight
  • State Street SPDR Russell 1000 Yield Focus ETF (NYSE:ONEY): 1.17% Weight
  • SPDR Russell 1000 Yield Focus ETF (NYSE:ONEY): 1.33% Weight

Significance: Because NKE carries meaningful weight in these funds, any significant inflows or outflows for these ETFs will likely force automatic buying or selling of the stock

Price Action

NKE Price Action: Nike shares were up 1.01% at $66.30 at the time of publication on Tuesday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

Whales are entities with large sums of money and we track their transactions here at Benzinga on our options activity scanner.

Options Alert Terminology
- Call Contracts: The right to buy shares as indicated in the contract.
- Put Contracts: The right to sell shares as indicated in the contract.

data.title: Dave Ramsey Says Claim Social Security Early — 'Might as Well Get All You Can' But Suze Orman Says Waiting Gets You 76% More Per Month. Who's Right?
data.description:
Retire early and cash in while you can — or wait and score a bigger monthly check? That's the divide between two financial powerhouses who have wildly different views on when to claim Social Security.

In a blog post from Ramsey Solutions, the article states that financial expert Dave Ramsey says it makes sense in most cases to take the money early and often. The logic is simple: Social Security benefits don't outlive you. As the blog explains, "Social Security payments die when you die, so you might as well get all you can get as fast as you can get it."

If you don't need the money immediately, the article suggests investing it in growth stock mutual funds to build long-term wealth. "If you take nothing, you get nothing," it adds — a blunt warning about the risks of delaying and receiving nothing if life doesn't go as planned.

Don't Miss:

  • The ‘ChatGPT of Marketing' Just Opened a $0.85/Share Round — 10,000+ Investors Are Already In
  • Warren Buffett once said, "If you don't find a way to make money while you sleep, you will work until you die." Here’s how you can earn passive income with just $10, starting today.

The blog also breaks down the math using a chart from CNBC News, showing how much of your full benefit you receive depending on your age. Ramsey Solutions shared the chart to show that:

  • At age 62, you receive 70% of your full benefit
  • At full retirement age, 67, you receive 100%
  • If you wait until 70, you receive 124% — a 76% boost over claiming early

So where does financial guru Suze Orman stand?

In a blog post on her site, Orman urges readers to do the opposite. She writes, "If you are in decent health, there is no better financial move to consider than waiting as long as possible to claim your Social Security benefit."

She backs her case with life expectancy data: a 65-year-old woman in average health has a 50% chance of living to 88, while a man has a 50% shot at reaching 85. Waiting until age 70 to claim benefits, she notes, leads to monthly payments that are 76% higher than if you started at age 62. Orman also points out that the break-even point — when you start coming out ahead for having waited — is around age 81.

In dollar terms, Ramsey Solutions gives the example of someone receiving $1,430 per month if they claim at 62, versus $2,535 per month if they wait until 70.

Ultimately, choosing when to claim depends on your health, income needs, and long-term plan. Those who need income earlier or aren't confident they'll live into their 80s may find Ramsey's approach more practical. Those with strong health and a longer runway may benefit from Orman's strategy.

Either way, the decision is permanent — and there's no one-size-fits-all. A financial adviser can help you compare scenarios based on your personal data, retirement goals, and how long you expect to live.

Because when it comes to Social Security, it's not about who's right. It's about what's right for you.

  • Invest in a Gaming Marketplace Backed by Early Zynga and PayPal Investors— From $300.
  • Wall Street's $12B Real Estate Manager Is Opening Its Doors to Individual Investors — Without the Crowdfunding Middlemen

Image: Imagn

data.title: JPMorgan Chase's Shares Nosedive After Q4 Results, This Analyst Remains Bullish
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase & Co (NYSE:JPM) reported its fourth-quarter earnings largely in line with expectations and guided to 2026 net interest income (NII) 2% stronger than consensus, according to Goldman Sachs.

• JPMorgan Chase shares are retreating from recent levels. What’s pressuring JPM stock?

The JPMorgan Chase Analyst: Goldman Sachs analyst Richard Ramsden reaffirmed a Buy rating and price target of $386.

The JPMorgan Chase Thesis: The bank reported earnings of $4.63 per share, beating consensus of $5.02 per share, while core earnings came in at $5.25 per share, almost in line with estimates of $5.22 per share, Ramsden said in the note.

Check out other analyst stock ratings.

Despite lower-than-expected revenues, JPMorgan reported in-line earnings on the back of better expenses, he added.  

The company witnessed strong loan growth trends, up 4% sequentially, which supported its above-expectations NII outlook, the analyst stated.

"Management reiterated the 2026 NII ex Markets guide of ~$95bn, and laid out an all in NII guidance to ~$103bn, which compared to GSe/Street of $101bn," he further wrote.

JPM Price Action: Shares of JPMorgan Chase had declined by 3.33% to $313.68 at the time of publication on Tuesday.

Photo: Shutterstock
